title: Death rate from neonatal preterm birth complications
subtitle: The estimated death rate from [neonatal](#dod:neonatal) [preterm birth complications](#dod:preterm-birth-complications) in children under five years old, per 100,000.
caption: Across the globe in 2023, the estimated death rate from neonatal preterm birth complications in children under five years old varies widely, measured per 100,000 population. The map uses a continuous color scale ranging from pale yellow for the lowest rates (near 0) to deep navy blue for the highest (approaching 1,000), with intermediate bands at 10, 20, 50, 100, 200, and 500. Countries in sub-Saharan Africa — including Nigeria, Democratic Republic of the Congo, Ethiopia, and Somalia — fall within the darkest blue bands, indicating rates between 200 and 1,000 per 100,000. In contrast, most of Western Europe — such as Germany, France, Spain, and the United Kingdom — along with Australia, Japan, and Canada, appear in pale yellow or light green, corresponding to rates below 20 per 100,000. Intermediate values are visible across much of Latin America — Brazil, Mexico, and Argentina sit in the 20–50 range — while parts of South Asia, including India and Pakistan, show moderate-to-high rates in the 50–200 band. Russia and China occupy mid-range shades, falling between 20 and 100. Greenland and several small island nations are marked “No data” with hatched patterns. The geographic distribution shows a clear concentration of higher mortality rates across equatorial and southern regions of Africa and parts of South Asia, while lower rates dominate North America, Europe, East Asia, and Oceania. The pattern visible on the map places the highest burden of neonatal preterm birth complications in low- and middle-income countries of Africa and South Asia, with the lowest rates clustered in high-income nations across multiple continents.
